## HOW NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE: FROM FEEDFORWARD TO GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS
**Keyulu Xu** _[†]_ **, Mozhi Zhang** _[‡]_ **, Jingling Li** _[‡]_ **, Simon S. Du** _[§]_ **, Ken-ichi Kawarabayashi** _[¶]_ **, Stefanie Jegelka** _[†]_

> _†_ Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)

> _‡_ University of Maryland

> _§_ University of Washington

> _¶_ National Institute of Informatics (NII)

keyulu@mit.edu, keyulux@csail.mit.edu

## ABSTRACT

We study how neural networks trained by gradient descent _extrapolate_ , i.e., what they learn outside the support of the training distribution. Previous works report mixed empirical results when extrapolating with neural networks: while feedforward neural networks, a.k.a. multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), do not extrapolate well in certain simple tasks, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) – structured networks with MLP modules – have shown some success in more complex tasks. Working towards a theoretical explanation, we identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate well. First, we quantify the observation that ReLU MLPs quickly converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin, which implies that ReLU MLPs do not extrapolate most nonlinear functions. But, they can provably learn a linear target function when the training distribution is sufficiently “diverse”. Second, in connection to analyzing the successes and limitations of GNNs, these results suggest a hypothesis for which we provide theoretical and empirical evidence: the success of GNNs in extrapolating algorithmic tasks to new data (e.g., larger graphs or edge weights) relies on encoding task-specific non-linearities in the architecture or features. Our theoretical analysis builds on a connection of over-parameterized networks to the neural tangent kernel. Empirically, our theory holds across different training settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Humans extrapolate well in many tasks. For example, we can apply arithmetics to arbitrarily large numbers. One may wonder whether a neural network can do the same and generalize to examples arbitrarily far from the training data (Lake et al., 2017). Curiously, previous works report mixed extrapolation results with neural networks. Early works demonstrate feedforward neural networks, a.k.a. multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), fail to extrapolate well when learning simple polynomial functions (Barnard & Wessels, 1992; Haley & Soloway, 1992). However, recent works show Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Scarselli et al., 2009), a class of structured networks with MLP building blocks, can generalize to graphs much larger than training graphs in challenging algorithmic tasks, such as predicting the time evolution of physical systems (Battaglia et al., 2016), learning graph algorithms (Velickovic et al., 2020), and solving mathematical equations (Lample & Charton, 2020).

To explain this puzzle, we formally study how neural networks trained by gradient descent (GD) extrapolate, i.e., what they learn outside the support of training distribution. We say a neural network extrapolates well if it learns a task outside the training distribution. At first glance, it may seem that neural networks can behave arbitrarily outside the training distribution since they have high capacity (Zhang et al., 2017) and are universal approximators (Cybenko, 1989; Funahashi, 1989; Hornik et al., 1989; Kurkova, 1992). However, neural networks are constrained by gradient descent training (Hardt et al., 2016; Soudry et al., 2018). In our analysis, we explicitly consider such implicit bias through the analogy of the training dynamics of over-parameterized neural networks and kernel regression via the _neural tangent kernel (NTK)_ (Jacot et al., 2018).

Figure 2: **How GNNs extrapolate.** Since MLPs can extrapolate well when learning linear functions, we hypothesize that GNNs can extrapolate well in dynamic programming (DP) tasks if we encode appropriate non-linearities in the architecture (left) and input representation (right; through domain knowledge or representation learning). The encoded non-linearities may not be necessary for interpolation, as they can be approximated by MLP modules, but they help extrapolation. We support the hypothesis theoretically (Theorem 3) and empirically (Figure 6).

Starting with feedforward networks, the simplest neural networks and building blocks of more complex architectures such as GNNs, we establish that the predictions of over-parameterized MLPs with ReLU activation trained by GD converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin. We prove a convergence rate for two-layer networks and empirically observe that convergence often occurs _close to_ the training data (Figure 1), which suggests ReLU MLPs cannot extrapolate well for most nonlinear tasks. We emphasize that our results do not follow from the fact that ReLU networks have finitely many linear regions (Arora et al., 2018; Hanin & Rolnick, 2019; Hein et al., 2019). While having finitely many linear regions implies ReLU MLPs _eventually_ become linear, it does not say whether MLPs will learn the correct target function close to the training distribution. In contrast, our results are non-asymptotic and quantify what kind of functions MLPs will learn close to the training distribution. Second, we identify a condition when MLPs extrapolate well: the task is linear and the geometry of the training distribution is sufficiently “diverse”. To our knowledge, our results are the first extrapolation results of this kind for feedforward neural networks.

We then relate our insights into feedforward neural networks to GNNs, to explain why GNNs extrapolate well in some algorithmic tasks. Prior works report successful extrapolation for tasks that can be solved by dynamic programming (DP) (Bellman, 1966), which has a computation structure aligned with GNNs (Xu et al., 2020). DP updates can often be decomposed into nonlinear and linear steps. Hence, we hypothesize that GNNs trained by GD can extrapolate well in a DP task, if we encode appropriate non-linearities in the _architecture_ and _input representation_ (Figure 2). Importantly, encoding non-linearities may be unnecessary for GNNs to _interpolate_ , because the MLP modules can easily learn many nonlinear functions inside the training distribution (Cybenko, 1989; Hornik et al., 1989; Xu et al., 2020), but it is crucial for GNNs to _extrapolate_ correctly. We prove this hypothesis for a simplified case using _Graph NTK_ (Du et al., 2019b). Empirically, we validate the hypothesis on three DP tasks: max degree, shortest paths, and _n_ -body problem. We show GNNs with appropriate architecture, input representation, and training distribution can predict well on graphs with unseen sizes, structures, edge weights, and node features. Our theory explains the empirical success in previous works and suggests their limitations: successful extrapolation relies on encoding task-specific non-linearities, which requires domain knowledge or extensive model search. From a broader standpoint, our insights go beyond GNNs and apply broadly to other neural networks.

To summarize, we study how neural networks extrapolate. First, ReLU MLPs trained by GD converge to linear functions along directions from the origin with a rate of _O_ (1 _/t_ ). Second, to explain why GNNs extrapolate well in some algorithmic tasks, we prove that ReLU MLPs can extrapolate well in linear tasks, leading to a hypothesis: a neural network can extrapolate well when appropriate nonlinearities are encoded into the architecture and features. We prove this hypothesis for a simplified case and provide empirical support for more general settings.

## 1.1 RELATED WORK

Early works show example tasks where MLPs do not extrapolate well, e.g. learning simple polynomials (Barnard & Wessels, 1992; Haley & Soloway, 1992). We instead show a _general_ pattern of how ReLU MLPs extrapolate and identify conditions for MLPs to extrapolate well. More recent works study the implicit biases induced on MLPs by gradient descent, for both the NTK and mean field regimes (Bietti & Mairal, 2019; Chizat & Bach, 2018; Song et al., 2018). Related to our results, some works show MLP predictions converge to “simple” piecewise linear functions, e.g., with few linear regions (Hanin & Rolnick, 2019; Maennel et al., 2018; Savarese et al., 2019; Williams et al., 2019). Our work differs in that none of these works explicitly studies extrapolation, and some focus only on one-dimensional inputs. Recent works also show that in high-dimensional settings of the NTK regime, MLP is asymptotically at most a linear predictor in certain scaling limits (Ba et al., 2020; Ghorbani et al., 2019). We study a different setting (extrapolation), and our analysis is non-asymptotic in nature and does not rely on random matrix theory.

Prior works explore GNN extrapolation by testing on larger graphs (Battaglia et al., 2018; Santoro et al., 2018; Saxton et al., 2019; Velickovic et al., 2020). We are the first to theoretically study GNN extrapolation, and we complete the notion of extrapolation to include unseen features and structures.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

We begin by introducing our setting. Let _X_ be the domain of interest, e.g., vectors or graphs. The task is to learn an underlying function _g_ : _X →_ R with a training set _{_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂D]_[,][where] _yi_ = _g_ ( _**x** i_ ) and _D_ is the support of training distribution. Previous works have extensively studied in-distribution generalization where the training and the test distributions are identical (Valiant, 1984; Vapnik, 2013); i.e., _D_ = _X_ . In contrast, extrapolation addresses predictions on a domain _X_ that is larger than the support of the training distribution _D_ . We will say a model _extrapolates well_ if it has a small _extrapolation error_ .

**Definition 1.** (Extrapolation error). Let _f_ : _X →_ R be a model trained on _{_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂D]_[ with] underlying function _g_ : _X →_ R. Let _P_ be a distribution over _X \ D_ and let _ℓ_ : R _×_ R _→_ R be a loss function. We define the extrapolation error of _f_ as E _**x** ∼P_ [ _ℓ_ ( _f_ ( _**x**_ ) _, g_ ( _**x**_ ))].
We focus on neural networks trained by _gradient descent_ (GD) or its variants with _squared loss_ . We study two network architectures: feedforward and graph neural networks.

**Graph Neural Networks.** GNNs are structured networks operating on graphs with MLP modules (Battaglia et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019). Let _G_ = ( _V, E_ ) be a graph. Each node _u ∈ V_ has a feature vector _**x** u_ , and each edge ( _u, v_ ) _∈ E_ has a feature vector _**w**_ ( _u,v_ ). GNNs recursively compute node representations _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] at iteration _k_ (Gilmer et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2018). Initially, _**h**_[(0)] _u_ = _**x** u_ . For _k_ = 1 _..K_ , GNNs update _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] by aggregating the neighbor representations. We can optionally compute a graph representation _**h** G_ by aggregating the final node representations. That is,

The final output is the graph representation _**h** G_ or final node representations _**h**_[(] _u[K]_[)] depending on the task. We refer to the neighbor aggregation step for _**h**_[(] _u[k]_[)] as _aggregation_ and the pooling step in _**h** G_ as _readout_ . Previous works typically use sum-aggregation and sum-readout (Battaglia et al., 2018). Our results indicate why replacing them may help extrapolation (Section 4).

## 3 HOW FEEDFORWARD NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE

Feedforward networks are the simplest neural networks and building blocks of more complex architectures such as GNNs, so we first study how they extrapolate when trained by GD. Throughout the paper, we assume ReLU activation. Section 3.3 contains preliminary results for other activations.

## 3.1 LINEAR EXTRAPOLATION BEHAVIOR OF RELU MLPS

By architecture, ReLU networks learn piecewise linear functions, but what do these regions precisely look like outside the support of the training data? Figure 1 illustrates examples of how ReLU MLPs extrapolate when trained by GD on various nonlinear functions. These examples suggest that outside the training support, the predictions quickly become linear along directions from the origin. We systematically verify this pattern by linear regression on MLPs’ predictions: the coefficient of determination ( _R_[2] ) is always greater than 0.99 (Appendix C.2). That is, ReLU MLPs “linearize” almost immediately outside the training data range.

We formalize this observation using the implicit biases of neural networks trained by GD via the _neural tangent kernel (NTK)_ : optimization trajectories of over-parameterized networks trained by GD are equivalent to those of kernel regression with a specific neural tangent kernel, under a set of assumptions called the “NTK regime” (Jacot et al., 2018). We provide an informal definition here; for further details, we refer the readers to Jacot et al. (2018) and Appendix A.
**Definition 2.** (Informal) A neural network trained in the _NTK regime_ is infinitely wide, randomly initialized with certain scaling, and trained by GD with infinitesimal steps.

Prior works analyze optimization and in-distribution generalization of over-parameterized neural networks via NTK (Allen-Zhu et al., 2019a;b; Arora et al., 2019a;b; Cao & Gu, 2019; Du et al., 2019c;a; Li & Liang, 2018; Nitanda & Suzuki, 2021). We instead analyze extrapolation.

Theorem 1 formalizes our observation from Figure 1: outside the training data range, along any direction _t_ _**v**_ from the origin, the prediction of a two-layer ReLU MLP quickly converges to a linear

ReLU networks have finitely many linear regions (Arora et al., 2018; Hanin & Rolnick, 2019), hence their predictions _eventually_ become linear. In contrast, Theorem 1 is a more fine-grained analysis of _how_ MLPs extrapolate and provides a convergence rate. While Theorem 1 assumes two-layer networks in the NTK regime, experiments confirm that the linear extrapolation behavior happens across networks with different depths, widths, learning rates, and batch sizes (Appendix C.1 and C.2). Our proof technique potentially also extends to deeper networks.

Theorem 1 implies which target functions a ReLU MLP may be able to match outside the training data: only functions that are almost-linear along the directions away from the origin. While learning linear functions may seem very limited at first, in Section 4 this insight will help explain extrapolation properties of GNNs in non-linear practical tasks. Before that, we first theoretically analyze when MLPs extrapolate well.

## 3.2 WHEN RELU MLPS PROVABLY EXTRAPOLATE WELL

Figure 4a shows that MLPs can extrapolate well when the target function is linear. However, this is not always true. In this section, we show that successful extrapolation depends on the _geometry_ of training data. Intuitively, the training distribution must be “diverse” enough for correct extrapolation.

We provide two conditions that relate the geometry of the training data to extrapolation. Lemma 1 states that over-parameterized MLPs can learn a linear target function with only 2 _d_ examples. **Lemma 1.** _Let g_ ( _**x**_ ) = _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** be the target function for_ _**β** ∈_ R _[d] . Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[contains][an] orthogonal basis {_ _**x**_ ˆ _i}[d] i_ =1 _[and][ {−]_ _**[x]**_[ˆ] _[i][}][d] i_ =1 _[.][If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP][ f][on][ {]_[(] _**[x]**[i][, y][i]_[)] _[}][n] i_ =1 _with squared loss in the NTK regime, then f_ ( _**x**_ ) = _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** for all_ _**x** ∈_ R _[d] ._

Lemma 1 is mainly of theoretical interest, as the 2 _d_ examples need to be carefully chosen. Theorem 2 builds on Lemma 1 and identifies a more practical condition for successful extrapolation: if the support of the training distribution covers all directions (e.g., a hypercube that covers the origin), the MLP converges to a linear target function with sufficient training data.

Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[is sampled from a distribution whose support][ D][contains a connected subset][ S][,] where for any non-zero_ _**w** ∈_ R _[d] , there exists k >_ 0 _so that k_ _**w** ∈S. If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP f_ : R _[d] →_ R _on {_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[with squared loss in the NTK regime,][ f]_[(] _**[x]**_[)] _−→p_ _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** as n →∞._

**Experiments: geometry of training data affects extrapolation.** The condition in Theorem 2 formalizes the intuition that the training distribution must be “diverse” for successful extrapolation, e.g., _D_ includes all directions. Empirically, the extrapolation error is indeed small when the condition of Theorem 2 is satisfied (“all” in Figure 4b). In contrast, the extrapolation error is much larger when the training examples are restricted to only some directions (Figure 4b and Figure 3).

Relating to previous works, Theorem 2 suggests why spurious correlations may hurt extrapolation, complementing the causality arguments (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Peters et al., 2016; Rojas-Carulla et al., 2018). When the training data has spurious correlations, some combinations of features are missing; e.g., camels might only appear in deserts in an image collection. Therefore, the condition for Theorem 2 no longer holds, and the model may extrapolate incorrectly. Theorem 2 is also analogous to an identifiability condition for linear models, but stricter. We can uniquely identify a linear function if the training data has full (feature) rank. MLPs are more expressive, so identifying the linear target function requires additional constraints.

Figure 5: **Extrapolation performance of MLPs with other activation** . MLPs can extrapolate well when the activation is “similar” to the target function. When learning quadratic with quadratic activation, 2-layer networks (quad-2) extrapolate well, but 4-layer networks (quad-4) do not.

To summarize, we analyze how ReLU MLPs extrapolate and provide two insights: (1) MLPs cannot extrapolate most nonlinear tasks due to their linear extrapolation (Theorem 1); and (2) MLPs extrapolate well when the target function is linear, if the training distribution is “diverse” (Theorem 2). In the next section, these results help us understand how more complex networks extrapolate.

## 3.3 MLPS WITH OTHER ACTIVATION FUNCTIONS

Before moving on to GNNs, we complete the picture of MLPs with experiments on other activation functions: tanh _σ_ ( _x_ ) = tanh( _x_ ), cosine _σ_ ( _x_ ) = cos( _x_ ) (Lapedes & Farber, 1987; McCaughan, 1997; Sopena & Alquezar, 1994), and quadratic _σ_ ( _x_ ) = _x_[2] (Du & Lee, 2018; Livni et al., 2014). Details are in Appendix C.4. MLPs extrapolate well when the activation and target function are similar; e.g., tanh activation extrapolates well when learning tanh, but not other functions (Figure 5). Moreover, each activation function has different limitations. To extrapolate the tanh function with tanh activation, the training data range has to be sufficiently wide. When learning a quadratic function with quadratic activation, only two-layer networks extrapolate well as more layers lead to higher-order polynomials. Cosine activations are hard to optimize for high-dimensional data, so we only consider one/two dimensional cosine target functions.

## 4 HOW GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS EXTRAPOLATE

Above, we saw that extrapolation in nonlinear tasks is hard for MLPs. Despite this limitation, GNNs have been shown to extrapolate well in some nonlinear algorithmic tasks, such as intuitive physics (Battaglia et al., 2016; Janner et al., 2019), graph algorithms (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020), and symbolic mathematics (Lample & Charton, 2020). To address this discrepancy, we build on our MLP results and study how GNNs trained by GD extrapolate.

## 4.1 HYPOTHESIS: LINEAR ALGORITHMIC ALIGNMENT HELPS EXTRAPOLATION

We start with an example: training GNNs to solve the shortest path problem. For this task, prior works observe that a modified GNN architecture with min-aggregation can generalize to graphs larger than those in the training set (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020):
We first provide an intuitive explanation (Figure 2a). Shortest path can be solved by the Bellman-Ford (BF) algorithm (Bellman, 1958) with the following update:

where _**w**_ ( _v, u_ ) is the weight of edge ( _v, u_ ), and _d_ [ _k_ ][ _u_ ] is the shortest distance to node _u_ within _k_ steps. The two equations can be easily aligned: GNNs simulate the BF algorithm if its MLP modules learn a linear function _d_ [ _k −_ 1][ _v_ ]+ _**w**_ ( _v, u_ ). Since MLPs can extrapolate linear tasks, this “alignment” may explain why min-aggregation GNNs can extrapolate well in this task.

For comparison, we can reason why we would not expect GNNs with the more commonly used sum-aggregation (Eqn. 1) to extrapolate well in this task. With sum-aggregation, the MLP modules need to learn a nonlinear function to simulate the BF algorithm, but Theorem 1 suggests that they will not extrapolate most nonlinear functions outside the training support.

We can generalize the above intuition to other algorithmic tasks. Many tasks where GNNs extrapolate well can be solved by dynamic programming (DP) (Bellman, 1966), an algorithmic paradigm with a recursive structure similar to GNNs’ (Eqn. 1) (Xu et al., 2020).

**Definition 3.** _Dynamic programming_ (DP) is a recursive procedure with updates

where Answer[ _k_ ][ _s_ ] is the solution to a sub-problem indexed by iteration _k_ and state _s_ , and DP-Update is a task-specific update function that solves the sub-problem based on the previous iteration.

From a broader standpoint, we hypothesize that: if we encode appropriate non-linearities into the model architecture and input representations so that the MLP modules only need to learn nearly linear steps, then the resulting neural network can extrapolate well.

> **Hypothesis 1.** (Linear algorithmic alignment). Let _f_ : _X →_ R be the underlying function and _N_ a neural network with _m_ MLP modules. Suppose there exist _m linear_ functions _{gi}[m] i_ =1[so that by] replacing _N_ ’s MLP modules with _gi_ ’s, _N_ simulates _f_ . Given _ϵ >_ 0, there exists _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂] D_ ⊊ _X_ so that _N_ trained on _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1[by GD with squared loss learns] _[f]_[ˆ][with] _[ ∥][f]_[ˆ] _[−][f][∥][< ϵ]_[.]

Our hypothesis builds on the algorithmic alignment framework of (Xu et al., 2020), which states that a neural network _interpolates_ well if the modules are “aligned” to easy-to-learn (possibly nonlinear) functions. Successful extrapolation is harder: the modules need to align with linear functions.

**Applications of linear algorithmic alignment.** In general, linear algorithmic alignment is not restricted to GNNs and applies broadly to neural networks. To satisfy the condition, we can encode appropriate nonlinear operations in the _architecture_ or _input representation_ (Figure 2). Learning DP algorithms with GNNs is one example of encoding non-linearity in the architecture (Battaglia et al., 2018; Corso et al., 2020). Another example is to encode log-and-exp transforms in the architecture to help extrapolate multiplication in arithmetic tasks (Trask et al., 2018; Madsen & Johansen, 2020). Neural symbolic programs take a step further and encode a library of symbolic operations to help extrapolation (Johnson et al., 2017; Mao et al., 2019; Yi et al., 2018).

For some tasks, it may be easier to change the input representation (Figure 2b). Sometimes, we can decompose the target function _f_ as _f_ = _g ◦ h_ into a feature embedding _h_ and a “simpler” target function _g_ that our model can extrapolate well. We can obtain _h_ via specialized features or feature transforms using _domain knowledge_ (Lample & Charton, 2020; Webb et al., 2020), or via _representation learning_ (e.g., BERT) with unlabeled out-of-distribution data in _X \ D_ (Chen et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020; Mikolov et al., 2013b; Peters et al., 2018). This brings a new perspective of how representations help extrapolation in various application areas. For example, in natural language processing, pretrained representations (Mikolov et al., 2013a; Wu & Dredze, 2019) and feature transformation using domain knowledge (Yuan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019) help models generalize across languages, a special type of extrapolation. In quantitative finance, identifying the right “factors” or features is crucial for deep learning models as the financial markets may frequently be in extrapolation regimes (Banz, 1981; Fama & French, 1993; Ross, 1976).

Linear algorithmic alignment explains successful extrapolation in the literature and suggests that extrapolation is harder in general: encoding appropriate non-linearity often requires domain expertise or model search. Next, we provide theoretical and empirical support for our hypothesis.

## 4.2 THEORETICAL AND EMPIRICAL SUPPORT

We validate our hypothesis on three DP tasks: max degree, shortest path, and _n_ -body problem, and prove the hypothesis for max degree. We highlight the role of graph structures in extrapolation.

**Theoretical analysis.** We start with a simple yet fundamental task: learning the max degree of a graph, a special case of DP with one iteration. As a corollary of Theorem 1, the commonly used sum-based GNN (Eqn. 1) cannot extrapolate well (proof in Appendix B.4).

Theorem 3 does not follow immediately from Theorem 2, because MLP modules in GNNs only receive indirect supervision. We analyze the _Graph NTK_ (Du et al., 2019b) to prove Theorem 3 in Appendix B.5. While Theorem 3 assumes identical node features, we empirically observe similar results for both identical and non-identical features (Figure 16 in Appendix).

**Interpretation of conditions.** The condition in Theorem 3 is analogous to that in Theorem 2. Both theorems require diverse training data, measured by _graph structure_ in Theorem 3 or _directions_ in Theorem 2. In Theorem 3, the condition is violated if all training graphs have the same max or min node degrees, e.g., when training data are from one of the following families: path, _C_ -regular graphs (regular graphs with degree _C_ ), cycle, and ladder.

**Experiments: architectures that help extrapolation.** We validate our theoretical analysis with two DP tasks: max degree and shortest path (details in Appendix C.5 and C.6). While previous works only test on graphs with different sizes (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020), we also test on graphs with unseen structure, edge weights and node features. The results support our theory. For max degree, GNNs with max-readout are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a), confirming Corollary 1 and Theorem 3. For shortest path, GNNs with min-readout and min-aggregation are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a).

Experiments confirm the importance of training graphs structure (Figure 7). Interestingly, the two tasks favor different graph structure. For max degree, as Theorem 3 predicts, GNNs extrapolate well when trained on trees, complete graphs, expanders, and general graphs, and extrapolation errors are

For shortest path, extrapolation errors follow a U-shaped curve as we change the _sparsity_ of training graphs (Figure 7b and Figure 18 in Appendix). Intuitively, models trained on sparse or dense graphs likely learn degenerative solutions.

**Experiments: representations that help extrapolation.** Finally, we show a good input representation helps extrapolation. We study the _n_ -body problem (Battaglia et al., 2016; Watters et al., 2017) (Appendix C.7), that is, predicting the time evolution of _n_ objects in a gravitational system. Following previous work, the input is a complete graph where the nodes are the objects (Battaglia et al., 2016). We train GNNs to predict the velocity of each object _u_ at time _t_ + 1. The true velocity _f_ ( _G_ ; _u_ ) for object _u_ is approximately

We instead use an improved representation _h_ ( _G_ ) to encode non-linearity. ## 5 CONNECTIONS TO OTHER OUT-OF-DISTRIBUTION SETTINGS

We discuss several related settings. Intuitively, from the viewpoint of our results above, methods in related settings may improve extrapolation by 1) learning useful non-linearities beyond the training data range and 2) mapping relevant test data to the training data range.

**Domain adaptation** studies generalization to a specific target domain (Ben-David et al., 2010; Blitzer et al., 2008; Mansour et al., 2009). Typical strategies adjust the training process: for instance, use unlabeled samples from the target domain to align the target and source distributions (Ganin et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). Using target domain data during training may induce useful non-linearities and may mitigate extrapolation by matching the target and source distributions, though the correctness of the learned mapping depends on the label distribution (Zhao et al., 2019).

**Self-supervised learning** on a large amount of unlabeled data can learn useful non-linearities beyond the labeled training data range (Chen et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Peters et al., 2018). Hence, our results suggest an explanation why pre-trained representations such as BERT improve out-of-distribution robustness (Hendrycks et al., 2020). In addition, self-supervised learning could map semantically similar data to similar representations, so some out-of-domain examples might fall inside the training distribution after the mapping.

**Invariant models** aim to learn features that respect specific invariances across multiple training distributions (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Rojas-Carulla et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). If the model indeed learns these invariances, which can happen in the linear case and when there are confounders or anti-causal variables (Ahuja et al., 2021; Rosenfeld et al., 2021), this may essentially increase the training data range, since variations in the invariant features may be ignored by the model.

**Distributional robustness** considers small adversarial perturbations of the data distribution, and ensures that the model performs well under these (Goh & Sim, 2010; Sagawa et al., 2020; Sinha et al., 2018; Staib & Jegelka, 2019). We instead look at more global perturbations. Still, one would expect that modifications that help extrapolation in general also improve robustness to local perturbations.

## 6 CONCLUSION

This paper is an initial step towards formally understanding how neural networks trained by gradient descent extrapolate. We identify conditions under which MLPs and GNNs extrapolate as desired. We also suggest an explanation how GNNs have been able to extrapolate well in complex algorithmic tasks: encoding appropriate non-linearity in architecture and features can help extrapolation. Our results and hypothesis agree with empirical results, in this paper and in the literature.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper tackles a timely and important question—how neural networks behave outside the training distribution—and offers a compelling bridge between theoretical analysis of MLPs and empirical observations about GNN extrapolation on algorithmic tasks. The core contributions are substantive: a non-asymptotic characterization of ReLU MLPs converging to linear functions along radial directions, a positive result showing MLPs can extrapolate linear targets given sufficiently "diverse" training geometry, and an extension via the Neural Tangent Kernel framework to explain why GNNs succeed on dynamic programming tasks when non-linearities are appropriately encoded in architecture or input representation. The framing is clear and the narrative arc from MLPs to GNNs is well-motivated, with Figures 1 and 2 effectively conveying the main intuitions. That said, several concerns warrant attention. First, the notion of a "sufficiently diverse" training distribution is central to the positive extrapolation result but is introduced only informally in the abstract and introduction; its practical verifiability and restrictiveness deserve careful scrutiny. Second, the reliance on the NTK/over-parameterized regime is a significant limitation, since it is well-documented that NTK dynamics do not fully capture feature learning in practical networks—the paper should more explicitly address the gap between this idealization and the GNNs deployed in the referenced empirical successes (e.g., Velickovic et al., 2020). Third, the hypothesis about GNN extrapolation depending on encoded non-linearities, while intuitive, risks being somewhat tautological unless the conditions for "appropriate" encoding are made precise and falsifiable; the theoretical result is acknowledged to cover only a simplified case, so the strength of the empirical validation across the three DP tasks will be critical. Finally, the distinction the authors draw from prior work on finite linear regions of ReLU networks is important and should be emphasized throughout, as readers may otherwise undervalue the non-asymptotic nature of the contribution.

comments.title: Theorem 1 statement contains a likely OCR/formatting issue with the convergence bound
comments.explanation: Reading the statement, the quantity bounded should be |(f(x_0 + h v) − f(x_0))/h − β_v| < ε, i.e., a slope-convergence bound. As rendered, the 'h' appears in an odd position that could be read as a subscript rather than as a divisor. This looks like an OCR artifact of the fraction bar in (f(x_0+hv)−f(x_0))/h, and the intended meaning is clear from the preceding sentence 'f(x_0+hv)−f(x_0) → β_v · h'. However, as written it is ambiguous: a reader who does not reconstruct the fraction could misread the bound. Flagging as minor because the intended statement is recoverable but the displayed inequality is not dimensionally/logically consistent as printed (the LHS would not go to 0 without dividing by h).

comments.title: Theorem 2 condition allows S to miss the origin, which may conflict with the informal 'covers origin' description
comments.quote: Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[is sampled from a distribution whose support][ D][contains a connected subset][ S][,] where for any non-zero_ _**w** ∈_ R _[d] , there exists k >_ 0 _so that k_ _**w** ∈S.
comments.explanation: The formal condition requires that for every non-zero direction w, some positive scalar multiple kw lies in S, and S is connected. The informal description in the preceding paragraph says 'the support of the training distribution covers all directions (e.g., a hypercube that covers the origin)'. A connected set S meeting every ray from the origin need not contain the origin itself (e.g., a thin connected shell around the origin, or a connected set wrapping around it). This is probably fine mathematically — the theorem's condition is what matters — but the parenthetical example 'hypercube that covers the origin' suggests a stronger geometric intuition than what the theorem formally requires, and conversely Lemma 1 requires both x̂_i and −x̂_i (directions on both sides). comments.title: Hypothesis 1 allows trivial training distributions
comments.quote: Given _ϵ >_ 0, there exists _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂] D_ ⊊ _X_ so that _N_ trained on _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1[by GD with squared loss learns] _[f]_[̂][with] _[ ∥][f]_[̂] _[−][f][∥][< ϵ]_[.]
comments.explanation: Reading the hypothesis carefully: it only requires the *existence* of a training set with support D strictly smaller than X such that the network learns f approximately. This is a very weak statement — it does not require the property to hold for generic or reasonable training distributions. Taken literally, this could be trivially satisfied if D is essentially all of X minus a measure-zero set. The statement also doesn't specify over what norm ‖·‖ is taken or the geometric conditions on D (which Theorem 2 showed are crucial — e.g., covering all directions). The informal prose says linear algorithmic alignment means the network 'can extrapolate well,' but the formal hypothesis as written is much weaker than that intent and omits the diversity condition on D that Section 3.2 identified as essential.

comments.title: Theorem 3 condition statement may be incomplete/ambiguous
comments.explanation: The theorem is stated for the max degree function g, but the condition involves both g (max degree) and g' (min degree) along with N_i^max and N_i^min. It's not immediately obvious why the min-degree function appears in a condition for learning the max-degree function. A careful reader might wonder if this reflects the symmetric structure of the Graph NTK analysis (where learning max requires the training data to also distinguish min-related quantities), or whether this is a typo. Given that the theorem says "Let g and g' be the max/min degree function, respectively" and then gives one joint condition, the theorem appears to cover both functions simultaneously under the same spanning condition. This is plausible but the phrasing "learn g" at the end is ambiguous about whether it learns both g and g' or just g.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, clearly written, and addresses an important and under-theorized question: what neural networks trained by gradient descent do outside the training distribution. Its main strength is the coherent narrative connecting known empirical failures of MLP extrapolation with successes of GNNs on algorithmic tasks, and the proposed explanation—ReLU MLPs tend toward linear behavior outside the data, so successful extrapolation requires either linear target structure or architectural/representational encoding of the relevant nonlinearities—is compelling and intuitively useful. The introduction does a good job distinguishing this claim from the trivial observation that ReLU networks are piecewise linear, and the combination of NTK-based theory with empirical validation across MLPs and GNNs appears promising. The most significant issues are scope and precision: “extrapolation” is defined rather broadly, and the results seem to concern particular regimes—over-parameterized networks, gradient descent/NTK dynamics, directions from the origin, and simplified GNN/DP settings—so the paper should be careful not to overstate conclusions for general neural networks or all forms of out-of-distribution generalization. Similarly, terms such as “sufficiently diverse” training distribution and “appropriate nonlinearities” are central but need crisp formalization and discussion of how one would identify them in practice. The GNN story is attractive but potentially less definitive than the MLP theory, since the theorem appears to support a simplified case while the broader claims rely on empirical evidence and domain-specific architectural choices. Overall, the paper appears high quality and conceptually significant, but its impact depends on how rigorously it delineates the limits of the theory and avoids presenting a plausible explanatory framework as a complete account of extrapolation in neural networks.

comments.title: Lample & Charton is cited as evidence for GNN/graph-size extrapolation despite not being a GNN result
comments.quote: However, recent works show Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) ... can generalize to graphs much larger than training graphs ... such as ... solving mathematical equations (Lample & Charton, 2020). / Despite this limitation, GNNs have been shown to extrapolate well in some nonlinear algorithmic tasks ... and symbolic mathematics (Lample & Charton, 2020).
comments.explanation: Both cited passages use Lample & Charton (2020) as part of the evidence that GNNs extrapolate on graph-size or algorithmic tasks. That paper is best known for sequence-to-sequence/Transformer models for symbolic mathematics, not GNNs, and the task is not naturally a graph-size extrapolation result. The citation may fit a broader claim about neural extrapolation on symbolic tasks, but it does not support the narrower GNN-specific claim as written.

comments.title: The prose generalizes Theorem 1 beyond its formal two-layer NTK setting
comments.quote: Starting with feedforward networks ... we establish that the predictions of over-parameterized MLPs with ReLU activation trained by GD converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin. / Theorem 1 implies ... only functions that are almost-linear along the directions away from the origin. / experiments confirm that the linear extrapolation behavior happens across networks with different depths, widths, learning rates, and batch sizes.
comments.explanation: The formal result is for a two-layer ReLU MLP in the NTK regime and concerns asymptotic behavior along rays. Several passages state or use the conclusion in a broader way, suggesting it applies to ReLU MLPs generally, to target-function limitations generally, or that selected experiments 'confirm' the behavior across architectural and optimization choices. The intended message is reasonable, and the paper often includes caveats, but the distinction between the theorem and empirical evidence should be maintained more explicitly.

comments.title: Theorem 1’s non-asymptotic/rate interpretation lacks uniformity clarifications
comments.quote: our results are non-asymptotic and quantify what kind of functions MLPs will learn close to the training distribution. / For any direction v ... f(x0 + hv) - f(x0) → βv · h for any h > 0 ... given ϵ > 0, for t = O(1/ϵ) ...
comments.explanation: The theorem gives an asymptotic or rate statement along fixed rays, with constants depending on the training data and direction. As written, the surrounding prose can be read as saying the linear regime is guaranteed to begin close to the training distribution, but the theorem does not provide a uniform bound relating the constants to the training-support boundary. Similarly, the convergence statement is ambiguous about whether the O(1/t) control is for each fixed h or uniform over h. Clarifying that the result is along fixed directions and fixed increments, with constants possibly depending on those choices, would avoid overreading the theorem.

comments.title: Theorem 2 does not specify the quantifier or mode of convergence over x
comments.quote: If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP f : R^d → R ... with squared loss in the NTK regime, f(x) →p β⊤x as n → ∞.
comments.explanation: The theorem leaves x free in the convergence-in-probability assertion. It is unclear whether the claim is pointwise for every fixed test point, uniform over R^d, uniform on compact sets, or in an expected-risk sense over some extrapolation distribution. These are materially different extrapolation claims, especially because the paper’s central concern is performance outside the training distribution. The theorem should state the intended quantifier and mode of convergence.

comments.title: Hypothesis 1 is mathematically underspecified as an extrapolation claim
comments.quote: Given ϵ > 0, there exists {(xi, f(xi))}ni=1 ⊂ D ⊊ X so that N trained on {(xi, f(xi))}ni=1 by GD with squared loss learns f̂ with ∥f̂ − f∥ < ϵ.
comments.explanation: Although labeled a hypothesis rather than a theorem, the statement leaves key mathematical choices unspecified. The norm is not defined, so it is unclear whether the guarantee is uniform on X, expected under a distribution, empirical, or something else. The statement also does not specify training-support diversity, optimization regime, width assumptions, probability of success, or whether the internal inputs to each MLP module satisfy the geometry needed for linear extrapolation. End-to-end supervision also need not identify the intended internal linear modules. These gaps make the hypothesis difficult to interpret precisely.

comments.title: Sum-aggregation discussion blurs an aggregation-level limitation with an MLP extrapolation limitation
comments.quote: With sum-aggregation, the MLP modules need to learn a nonlinear function to simulate the BF algorithm, but Theorem 1 suggests that they will not extrapolate most nonlinear functions outside the training support.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that sum aggregation is poorly aligned with Bellman-Ford-style shortest-path computation is plausible. However, the mechanism is ambiguous. The Bellman-Ford update requires a set-level minimum over neighbors, while a per-edge MLP followed by summation generally cannot implement that minimum over arbitrary variable-size neighborhoods. Thus the issue is not merely that an MLP must learn a nonlinear scalar function outside its support. If additional post-aggregation nonlinear processing is intended, the architecture should be stated more clearly.

comments.title: Causal explanation for deeper quadratic-activation failure is overstated
comments.quote: When learning a quadratic function with quadratic activation, only two-layer networks extrapolate well as more layers lead to higher-order polynomials.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is plausible: deeper quadratic-activation networks can represent higher-degree polynomials and may extrapolate according to those higher-degree terms. But the causal wording is too strong. Such networks can still represent lower-degree polynomials through parameter choices, cancellations, or degenerate layers. The statement would be safer if framed as an observed implicit-bias tendency rather than as an architectural necessity.

comments.title: Theorem 3’s diversity condition is paraphrased too weakly
comments.quote: In max degree, GNNs with max readout extrapolate well if the max/min degrees of the training graphs are not restricted (Theorem 3).
comments.explanation: Theorem 3 requires a stronger linear-algebraic spanning condition on vectors involving graph statistics and max/min degree counts. Merely having unrestricted or varying max/min degrees is not equivalent to that condition. For example, complete graphs of varying sizes can have varying degrees while still producing vectors confined to a lower-dimensional subspace. The prose therefore overstates what Theorem 3 guarantees.

comments.title: Shortest-path architecture comparison blurs aggregation and readout
comments.quote: For shortest path, GNNs with min-readout and min-aggregation are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a).
comments.explanation: For shortest paths, the Bellman-Ford-aligned operation is the minimum over incoming candidate distances, so the aggregation operator is crucial. The sentence describes the improved model as using both min-readout and min-aggregation, but describes the baseline only as using sum-readout. If the baseline also uses sum-aggregation, that should be stated. Otherwise, the comparison may obscure which architectural component drives the improvement.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of what invariant models ignore
comments.quote: this may essentially increase the training data range, since variations in the invariant features may be ignored by the model.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'variations in the invariant features' is easy to misread. In invariant-learning or domain-generalization settings, the usual goal is to rely on stable predictive features and ignore nuisance, spurious, or environment-specific variation. If a feature is genuinely invariant and predictive, ignoring its variation is not usually the intended mechanism. The sentence should distinguish invariant predictive structure from non-invariant or nuisance variation discarded by the model.

comments.title: Speculative link from extrapolation improvements to local robustness is overstated
comments.quote: Still, one would expect that modifications that help extrapolation in general also improve robustness to local perturbations.
comments.explanation: The relationship between extrapolation and local robustness is plausible in some cases but not guaranteed. A task-specific architectural bias may improve extrapolation along intended out-of-support directions while leaving the model brittle to small perturbations in other directions; robust optimization can also trade off with other forms of generalization. Since this appears in a related-work or concluding discussion, it is not a serious flaw, but the claim should be qualified.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, clearly written, and addresses an important and under-theorized question: what neural networks trained by gradient descent do outside the training distribution. Its main strength is the coherent narrative connecting known empirical failures of MLP extrapolation with successes of GNNs on algorithmic tasks, and the proposed explanation—ReLU MLPs tend toward linear behavior outside the data, so successful extrapolation requires either linear target structure or architectural/representational encoding of the relevant nonlinearities—is compelling and intuitively useful. The introduction does a good job distinguishing this claim from the trivial observation that ReLU networks are piecewise linear, and the combination of NTK-based theory with empirical validation across MLPs and GNNs appears promising. The most significant issues are scope and precision: “extrapolation” is defined rather broadly, and the results seem to concern particular regimes—over-parameterized networks, gradient descent/NTK dynamics, directions from the origin, and simplified GNN/DP settings—so the paper should be careful not to overstate conclusions for general neural networks or all forms of out-of-distribution generalization. Similarly, terms such as “sufficiently diverse” training distribution and “appropriate nonlinearities” are central but need crisp formalization and discussion of how one would identify them in practice. The GNN story is attractive but potentially less definitive than the MLP theory, since the theorem appears to support a simplified case while the broader claims rely on empirical evidence and domain-specific architectural choices. Overall, the paper appears high quality and conceptually significant, but its impact depends on how rigorously it delineates the limits of the theory and avoids presenting a plausible explanatory framework as a complete account of extrapolation in neural networks.

comments.title: Example cited as GNN extrapolation does not appear to be a GNN/graph-size result
comments.quote: However, recent works show Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Scarselli et al., 2009), a class of structured networks with MLP building blocks, can generalize to graphs much larger than training graphs in challenging algorithmic tasks, such as predicting the time evolution of physical systems (Battaglia et al., 2016), learning graph algorithms (Velickovic et al., 2020), and solving mathematical equations (Lample & Charton, 2020).
comments.explanation: The intent is clear: the authors are motivating a contrast between poor extrapolation by plain MLPs and more successful extrapolation by structured neural architectures. The Battaglia et al. and Veličković et al. examples fit this framing well, since graph networks/GNN-style architectures are central there. I initially wondered whether the Lample & Charton example was meant only as a broader neural-symbolic extrapolation example, but the sentence specifically says that GNNs can generalize to much larger graphs and then lists solving mathematical equations as an example. Lample & Charton (2020) is best known for sequence-to-sequence/Transformer models for symbolic mathematics, not GNNs, and the task is not naturally described as generalizing to larger graphs. This is therefore a localized but real mismatch between the cited evidence and the claim being made. A small rephrasing separating broader neural extrapolation examples from GNN examples would resolve it.

comments.title: The stated MLP extrapolation claim is broader than the formal theorem later supports
comments.quote: Starting with feedforward networks, the simplest neural networks and building blocks of more complex architectures such as GNNs, we establish that the predictions of over-parameterized MLPs with ReLU activation trained by GD converge to linear functions along any direction from the origin.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paragraph partly resolves the concern: the next sentence says that the convergence rate is proved for two-layer networks and that broader behavior is observed empirically. In the full context, Theorem 1 is indeed restricted to a two-layer ReLU MLP in the NTK regime, i.e. an idealized infinite-width/infinitesimal-step setting. The quoted sentence, however, reads as if the paper establishes the statement for over-parameterized ReLU MLPs trained by GD in general, including deeper finite-width networks and ordinary finite-step GD/SGD. Since the distinction between NTK-regime theory and empirical finite-network behavior is technically important in this area, the prose slightly overstates the formal result. This is fixable by saying, for example, that the authors prove this in the two-layer NTK setting and empirically observe it more broadly.

comments.title: “Close to the training distribution” is stronger than what the theorem alone guarantees
comments.quote: In contrast, our results are non-asymptotic and quantify what kind of functions MLPs will learn close to the training distribution.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: finite linear-region arguments only imply eventual linearity, while the authors’ NTK analysis gives a rate along rays, and their experiments suggest linearization can happen soon after leaving the training range. The formal result in the full context does provide an O(1/t)-type convergence statement, so calling the analysis non-asymptotic is not baseless. What remains potentially misleading is the phrase “close to the training distribution.” The theorem’s constants depend on the training data and direction, and the statement is along rays as t grows; without a uniform bound relating those constants to the scale or boundary of the training support, the theorem by itself does not guarantee that the linear regime begins close to the training data. The empirical observations may support this, but the sentence attributes it to “our results” without distinguishing theorem from experiment.

comments.title: Theorem 1 convergence statement is ambiguous about dependence on the step length h
comments.quote: function with rate _O_ ([1] _t_[)][.][The linear coefficients] _**[ β][v]**_[and the constant terms in the convergence rate] depend on the training data and direction _**v**_ . The proof is in Appendix B.1. **Theorem 1.** _(Linear extrapolation). Suppose we train a two-layer ReLU MLP f_ : R _[d] →_ R _with squared loss in the NTK regime. For any direction_ _**v** ∈_ R _[d] , let_ _**x**_ 0 = _t_ _**v** . As t →∞, f_ ( _**x**_ 0 + _h_ _**v**_ ) _− f_ ( _**x**_ 0) _→ β_ _**v** · h for any h >_ 0 _, where β_ _**v** is a constant linear coefficient. Moreover, given ϵ >_ 0 _, for t_ = _O_ ([1] _ϵ_[)] _[, we have][ |][f]_[(] _**[x]**_[0][+] _[h]_ _**[v]** h_[)] _[−][f]_[(] _**[x]**_[0][)] _− β_ _**v** | < ϵ._
comments.explanation: Setting aside the apparent OCR artifacts in the final displayed inequality, the intended result is clear enough: along a fixed ray, increments of the NTK-limit predictor approach an affine slope. My concern is with the quantification and the prose around the rate. The theorem says the convergence holds for any h > 0 and then summarizes the rate as t = O(1/ϵ), while the preceding sentence says the constants depend on the training data and direction v. For a fixed h this is a standard and plausible statement; however, as written it can be read as uniform over all h, with constants independent of h. That would be much stronger and generally not implied by an asymptotic local-increment result along a ray. If h is allowed to grow with t, or if one wants a whole long extrapolation interval rather than a fixed displacement, the stated O(1/t) control need not apply without additional uniformity assumptions. A small clarification such as “for each fixed h, with constants possibly depending on h” would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Theorem 2 does not specify the quantifier or mode of convergence over x
comments.quote: **Theorem 2.** _(Conditions for extrapolation). Let g_ ( _**x**_ ) = _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** be the target function for_ _**β** ∈_ R _[d] . Suppose {_ _**x** i}[n] i_ =1 _[is sampled from a distribution whose support][ D][contains a connected subset][ S][,] where for any non-zero_ _**w** ∈_ R _[d] , there exists k >_ 0 _so that k_ _**w** ∈S. If we train a two-layer ReLU MLP f_ : R _[d] →_ R _on {_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}[n] i_ =1 _[with squared loss in the NTK regime,][ f]_[(] _**[x]**_[)] _−→p_ _**β**[⊤]_ _**x** as n →∞._
comments.explanation: The intended claim is understandable from the surrounding discussion: with sufficiently diverse training support, the NTK-limit ReLU MLP recovers the linear target. But the formal statement leaves x free in the convergence-in-probability assertion. A reader has to infer whether this means convergence for every fixed test point x, uniformly over x in R^d, uniformly on compact sets, or in expectation over some extrapolation distribution. These are materially different claims. Pointwise convergence for each fixed x is much weaker than “the MLP converges to a linear target function” in a global extrapolation sense, while uniform or distributional convergence would require additional argument. Since the paper’s central notion is extrapolation error over a distribution outside D, the theorem would be clearer if it stated the exact quantifier and whether the convergence is pointwise, uniform, or in an Lp/extrapolation-risk sense.

comments.title: The implication from Theorem 1 to allowable target functions is stated somewhat more broadly than the theorem supports
comments.quote: Theorem 1 implies which target functions a ReLU MLP may be able to match outside the training data: only functions that are almost-linear along the directions away from the origin.
comments.explanation: I see the authors’ intended message: if the learned ReLU MLP/NTK predictor becomes affine along rays, then it cannot match a genuinely nonlinear target far outside the training range. That is a useful and largely correct intuition. What remains slightly problematic is that Theorem 1, as stated, is for two-layer ReLU MLPs in the NTK regime and concerns asymptotic fixed-step increments along rays from the origin. The prose here shifts to “a ReLU MLP” generally and sounds close to a necessary condition on target functions in all extrapolation settings. In practice, the extrapolation distribution might be bounded, might lie in directions not covered by the asymptotic regime, or might involve targets that are asymptotically affine along relevant rays even if nonlinear elsewhere. The phrase “may be able” softens this, so this is not a fatal issue, but the conclusion would be more precise if explicitly tied to the two-layer NTK setting and to asymptotic extrapolation along fixed rays.

comments.title: Experimental evidence is described as confirming behavior across architectures and hyperparameters
comments.quote: While Theorem 1 assumes two-layer networks in the NTK regime, experiments confirm that the linear extrapolation behavior happens across networks with different depths, widths, learning rates, and batch sizes (Appendix C.1 and C.2).
comments.explanation: The caveat at the start of the sentence is good: the formal theorem has a narrower setting than the empirical networks. My concern is the word “confirm.” Experiments over selected depths, widths, learning rates, and batch sizes can provide evidence that the phenomenon is robust beyond the theorem’s assumptions, but they do not confirm the behavior across those classes in the mathematical sense. This matters because the paper is using the empirical observation to generalize from a two-layer NTK theorem to practical MLPs. A wording such as “provide evidence that” or “we observe that” would better match what the experiments can establish.

comments.title: Example list attributes a non-GNN result to GNN extrapolation
comments.quote: Despite this limitation, GNNs have been shown to extrapolate well in some nonlinear algorithmic tasks, such as intuitive physics (Battaglia et al., 2016; Janner et al., 2019), graph algorithms (Battaglia et al., 2018; Velickovic et al., 2020), and symbolic mathematics (Lample & Charton, 2020).
comments.explanation: The general motivation is clear, and several of the cited works are indeed graph-network or graph-algorithm settings. However, I checked the particular examples against the surrounding claim: Lample & Charton (2020) is best known for sequence-to-sequence/Transformer models for symbolic mathematics, not for GNNs. If the authors intended to point to neural networks more broadly extrapolating on algorithmic tasks, the citation fits the broader narrative; but the sentence specifically says that “GNNs have been shown” to do this. That could mislead readers about the evidence base for GNN extrapolation.

comments.title: Hypothesis 1 is mathematically underspecified as an extrapolation claim
comments.quote: > **Hypothesis 1.** (Linear algorithmic alignment). Let _f_ : _X →_ R be the underlying function and _N_ a neural network with _m_ MLP modules. Suppose there exist _m linear_ functions _{gi}[m] i_ =1[so that by] replacing _N_ ’s MLP modules with _gi_ ’s, _N_ simulates _f_ . Given _ϵ >_ 0, there exists _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1 _[⊂] D_ ⊊ _X_ so that _N_ trained on _{_ ( _xi, f_ ( _xi_ )) _}[n] i_ =1[by GD with squared loss learns] _[f]_[ˆ][with] _[ ∥][f]_[ˆ] _[−][f][∥][< ϵ]_[.]
comments.explanation: I understand this is explicitly labeled a hypothesis rather than a theorem, and the surrounding prose is trying to distill the intuition from the MLP results: if the hard nonlinearities are built into the architecture or representation, the learned modules only need to extrapolate linearly. Still, the formal statement leaves several key mathematical choices unspecified. The norm \(\|\hat f-f\|\) is not defined: it could mean uniform error on all of \(X\), an expected extrapolation error under some distribution, an empirical norm, or something else. The statement also quantifies only that there exists a training set inside \(D\subsetneq X\), without specifying support/diversity conditions analogous to Theorem 2, optimization regime, width assumptions, probability of success, or whether the internal inputs to each MLP module satisfy the geometry needed for linear extrapolation. Finally, end-to-end supervision of \(f\) does not by itself ensure that the individual MLP modules learn the intended linear \(g_i\)'s, since internal decompositions can be non-identifiable. These gaps make the hypothesis difficult to interpret as a precise extrapolation claim, even allowing for its conjectural role.

comments.title: Sum-aggregation discussion blurs an aggregation-level limitation with an MLP extrapolation limitation
comments.quote: With sum-aggregation, the MLP modules need to learn a nonlinear function to simulate the BF algorithm, but Theorem 1 suggests that they will not extrapolate most nonlinear functions outside the training support.
comments.explanation: The intuition is reasonable: replacing the Bellman-Ford minimum by a standard sum aggregator removes the task-specific nonlinearity, so one should not expect robust extrapolation. However, I am not fully convinced by the way this is connected to Theorem 1. In the Bellman-Ford update, the hard operation is the set-level minimum over neighbors, \(\min_v(d_v^{(k-1)}+w(v,u))\), whereas Eqn. 1-style sum aggregation is additive over independently transformed messages. A per-edge MLP followed only by summation generally cannot exactly implement a minimum over arbitrary variable-size neighborhoods; the issue is not merely that the MLP must learn a nonlinear scalar function outside its support. Conversely, if the intended architecture includes additional post-aggregation nonlinear processing, that should be stated, because then the appeal to Theorem 1 is only a heuristic about that MLP’s extrapolation behavior. The conclusion may be right, but the mechanism is slightly ambiguous.

comments.title: Causal explanation for deeper quadratic-activation failure is overstated
comments.quote: When learning a quadratic function with quadratic activation, only two-layer networks extrapolate well as more layers lead to higher-order polynomials.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is plausible and useful: composing quadratic activations naturally creates high-degree polynomial terms, so deeper quadratic networks may extrapolate according to higher-degree polynomials rather than the target quadratic. What remains too strong is the causal wording “as more layers lead to higher-order polynomials.” A deeper quadratic-activation network can in principle still represent lower-degree polynomials through parameter choices, biases, cancellations, or degenerate layers; the mere presence of higher-degree terms in the function class does not by itself imply that deeper networks cannot extrapolate quadratics. The sentence would be safer if framed as an empirical observation or an implicit-bias tendency rather than a direct architectural necessity.

comments.title: Theorem 3’s diversity condition is paraphrased too weakly
comments.quote: In max degree, GNNs with max readout extrapolate well if the max/min degrees of the training graphs are not restricted (Theorem 3).
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear: as in Theorem 2, successful extrapolation depends on sufficiently diverse training structure, and max/min degree variation is part of that diversity. However, Theorem 3 as stated requires a substantially stronger linear-algebraic condition: the vectors \((g(G_i), g'(G_i), g(G_i)\cdot N_i^{\max}, g'(G_i)\cdot N_i^{\min})\) must span \(\mathbb R^4\). Merely having non-constant max and min degrees is not equivalent to this. For example, complete graphs of varying sizes have varying max/min degree, but always satisfy \(g(G_i)=g'(G_i)\) and \(N_i^{\max}=N_i^{\min}=|V_i|\), so their vectors lie in a lower-dimensional subspace and cannot span \(\mathbb R^4\). This matters because the surrounding discussion later says that success on complete graphs is predicted by Theorem 3. The empirical observation may still be correct, and the theorem may be only sufficient rather than necessary, but the prose overstates what the theorem actually guarantees.

comments.title: Shortest-path architecture comparison blurs aggregation and readout
comments.quote: For shortest path, GNNs with min-readout and min-aggregation are better than GNNs with sum-readout (Figure 6a).
comments.explanation: The broader context makes the intended algorithmic alignment plausible: shortest-path Bellman-Ford-style updates require a min over incoming candidate distances, so encoding min aggregation is the relevant architectural change. The paper also earlier distinguishes aggregation from readout, which helps interpret the sentence. Still, the comparison as written is ambiguous: the improved model is described as using both min-readout and min-aggregation, while the baseline is described only as using sum-readout. If the baseline also uses sum-aggregation, that should be stated, because for shortest paths the aggregation operator is the crucial DP-aligned nonlinearity. If instead only the readout differs, the claimed connection to the Bellman-Ford update would be much less direct. This is likely clarified in the appendix, but in the main text the wording could mislead readers about which architectural component drives the extrapolation improvement.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of what invariant models ignore
comments.quote: this may essentially increase the training data range, since variations in the invariant features may be ignored by the model.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that invariant learning helps because the model ignores variation in the features that are invariant. In the usual invariant-risk-minimization/domain-generalization framing, however, the goal is often to rely on stable/causal features and ignore variations in non-invariant, spurious, or environment-specific features. Context partly resolves the authors' intent: they are contrasting extrapolation with methods that exploit invariances across environments, and the point seems to be that invariance can make some distribution shifts irrelevant to the predictor. Still, the phrase 'variations in the invariant features' is easy to misread. If a feature is genuinely predictive and invariant in its relationship to the label, ignoring its variation would generally not be the intended mechanism. A clearer statement would distinguish invariant predictive features from nuisance or non-invariant variations that the learned representation discards.

comments.title: Speculative link from extrapolation improvements to local robustness
comments.quote: Still, one would expect that modifications that help extrapolation in general also improve robustness to local perturbations.
comments.explanation: The surrounding paragraph correctly distinguishes distributional robustness, which often concerns worst-case performance under bounded distribution shifts, from the paper's focus on more global extrapolation. My concern is with the final sentence: the implication from better extrapolation to better local robustness is plausible in some cases but not generally guaranteed. A modification that encodes a task-specific nonlinearity or architectural bias could improve extrapolation along the intended out-of-support directions while leaving the model brittle to small adversarial or distributional perturbations in other directions; conversely, robust optimization can sometimes hurt average or extrapolative performance. Since this is phrased as an expectation rather than a theorem, and appears in a related-work/conclusion-style discussion, it is not a serious flaw. But without qualification it slightly overstates the relationship between extrapolation and distributional robustness.
